segments.text: Could you tell me where the nearest library is?
Would you like some coffee or tea?
Do you know what time the meeting starts?
I'm sorry I'm late.
Can I borrow your pen for a moment?
How was your weekend?
I was hoping to return this jacket, but I've lost the receipt.
Have you had a chance to look over my report yet?
Do you think we should take the highway or the back roads?
I'm not sure I understand this assignment.
It looks like the printer's out of paper again.
Would it be possible to reschedule our appointment for next week?
I'd have joined you for the study group, but I was completely swamped with lab work.
Honestly, I can't decide whether the internship is worth giving up my summer for.
I'd assumed the deadline was flexible, but apparently it's set in stone.
If only I'd known the lecture was cancelled, I wouldn't have rushed across campus.
Hi, I'm working on a research paper about coral reefs, and the book I need isn't on the shelf. Could you help me track it down?
Of course. Let me check the catalog. It looks like that title is currently checked out and isn't due back for another two weeks.
Two weeks? My paper is due in ten days. Is there any way to get it sooner?
We can request it through interlibrary loan from one of our partner campuses. That usually takes about three to five business days.
That would work. Do I need to pay anything for that service?
There's no charge for students, but you'll need your library card and a valid email address so we can notify you when it arrives.
Great. While I'm here, are there any other resources you'd recommend on marine ecosystems?
Definitely. Our database of scientific journals has dozens of peer-reviewed articles on reef biology. I can show you how to search it before you go.
Professor Hale, thanks for seeing me. I've been struggling to narrow down a topic for my dissertation.
That's a common hurdle. What broad area are you drawn to at the moment?
I'm fascinated by urban migration, but every angle I consider feels like it's already been covered.
The fact that a subject has been studied doesn't mean it's exhausted. The trick is to find a gap—something the existing research overlooks or contradicts.
I see. I did notice that most studies focus on large cities, but hardly anyone looks at how migration reshapes smaller towns.
Now that's promising. A comparative study of two mid-sized towns could yield genuinely original findings, and the data would be far more manageable.
But would I be able to gather enough primary data in just one semester?

If you rely partly on census records and supplement them with a handful of interviews, yes. I'd caution you against attempting a survey of hundreds of residents—that's a project for a doctoral thesis, not a master's.
That's really helpful. I'll draft a proposal around the two-town comparison and send it to you by Friday.

Good afternoon, everyone. This is an announcement from the campus recreation center. Please be aware that the main swimming pool will be closed for maintenance from Monday through Wednesday of next week. During this time, the fitness rooms, indoor track, and basketball courts will remain open as usual. Members who have reserved swim lessons for those days will automatically be moved to the following week, and no fees will be charged for the rescheduling. If you have any questions, our front-desk staff will be happy to assist you. We apologize for any inconvenience and thank you for your understanding.

Attention, students. The annual Career and Internship Fair will take place this Thursday in the Student Union ballroom from ten in the morning until four in the afternoon. More than sixty employers from a range of industries will be on hand to discuss job openings, internships, and summer placements. We encourage you to bring several printed copies of your resume and to dress professionally. A resume-review station will be set up near the entrance, where career counselors can give you quick feedback before you meet with recruiters. Admission is free, and no advance registration is required. We look forward to seeing you there.

Today I want to talk about bioluminescence—the ability of living organisms to produce their own light. You've probably seen fireflies blinking on a summer evening, but the phenomenon is far more common in the ocean than on land. In fact, the majority of bioluminescent species live in the deep sea, where sunlight never reaches. So how does an animal generate light? The process relies on a chemical reaction between a molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase. When luciferin reacts with oxygen, it releases energy in the form of light. What's remarkable is that this reaction is extremely efficient—almost all of the energy becomes light rather than heat, which is why we call it cold light. Now, why would an organism evolve this ability? The functions vary widely. Some deep-sea fish use light to attract prey, dangling a glowing lure in front of their mouths. Others use it for defense—a sudden flash can startle a predator. And many species use light to find mates in the darkness. Understanding these mechanisms not only reveals how life adapts to extreme environments, but has also inspired new medical imaging techniques.

In this session, we'll examine one of the most consequential inventions in human history: Johannes Gutenberg's printing press, developed in the mid-fifteenth century. Now, printing itself was not entirely new—block printing had existed in East Asia for centuries. Gutenberg's true innovation was movable type: individual metal letters that could be rearranged and reused to set any page of text. The implications were staggering. Before the press, books were copied by hand, a laborious process that made them scarce and extraordinarily expensive. A single manuscript could take a scribe months to produce. Within decades of Gutenberg's invention, print shops had spread across Europe, and the number of books in circulation exploded from thousands to millions. But the significance goes beyond mere quantity. Cheaper books meant wider literacy, and wider literacy meant that ideas could circulate faster than any authority could control them. Historians often argue that the printing press was indispensable to the Protestant Reformation, since reformers could distribute their arguments to a mass audience. Likewise, the standardization of printed texts accelerated scientific progress, allowing scholars across the continent to compare precise observations. In short, the press did not merely reproduce knowledge—it transformed how knowledge itself was created and shared.
The library closes early on Sunday afternoons.
My brother usually walks to school before eight.
The professor asked us to submit our essays by Friday morning without exception.
Many students prefer studying in groups because they can share ideas quickly.
The museum recently opened a new exhibit about ancient sea creatures.
Although the weather was cold and windy, the volunteers kept planting trees along the river until sunset.
Scientists believe that regular exercise not only strengthens the body but also improves memory, mood, and the ability to concentrate.
What is your favorite way to relax after a long day, and why?
Do you prefer spending your free time indoors or outdoors? Explain your choice.
Describe a person who has had a positive influence on your life.
If you could learn one new skill this year, what would it be and why?
Excuse me, do you know if this bus goes downtown?
What are you planning to do this evening?
Could you pass me the salt, please?
Is it going to rain tomorrow?
Thanks so much for helping me move all those boxes.
Do you want to grab lunch after class?
I've been trying to reach the professor all week, but she never answers her email.
Would you mind if I turned the air conditioning down a bit?
I can't decide between the morning and the evening section of the course.
Our flight's been delayed by three hours.
Do you happen to know if the museum is open on Mondays?
I'm worried I won't finish the group project in time.
I would've signed up for the field trip, but it clashes with my part-time job.
Frankly, I'm not convinced that switching my major this late is a wise move.
Had I realized the tickets would sell out so quickly, I'd have booked them weeks ago.
I'm torn—the scholarship would really help, but the application takes ages to complete.
Hi, I'm interested in the study-abroad program in Spain, but I'm not sure I meet the requirements. Could you walk me through them?
Certainly. The main requirement is that you've completed at least two semesters of Spanish, and that you have a grade point average of three point zero or higher.
I've finished three semesters of Spanish, and my average is three point four, so I think I'm fine there. What about the cost?
The program fee covers tuition and housing, but not your airfare or meals. However, most students qualify for a travel grant that covers about half the flight.
That's a relief. How competitive is the application? I'd hate to get my hopes up for nothing.
It's selective, but not impossibly so. Last year, roughly two-thirds of applicants were accepted. A strong personal essay makes a real difference.
Good to know. When's the deadline, and what exactly do I need to submit?
Applications are due the first of March. You'll need the online form, your essay, and one letter of recommendation from a language instructor.
Professor Nunez, you asked me to come in about my lab report. I take it there were some problems with it?
Nothing fatal, but your conclusions overstate what the data actually support. You claim the reaction rate doubled, yet your own measurements show a much smaller increase.
I see. I suppose I was expecting it to double based on the textbook, so I may have read the results through that lens.
That's a classic trap—letting the hypothesis colour how you interpret the numbers. In science, the data have to lead, not the other way around.
So should I redo the experiment to get cleaner figures?

Not necessarily. The figures aren't wrong; your interpretation is. Rewrite the discussion so it reflects the modest increase you genuinely observed, and speculate on why it fell short of the prediction.
That makes sense. Could the temperature fluctuations in the lab have affected the rate?
Quite possibly, and that's exactly the sort of thing worth mentioning. Acknowledging sources of error strengthens a report rather than weakening it.
Understood. I'll revise the discussion section and resubmit it by next Tuesday.

Good morning. This is an important update from the university library. Beginning next Monday, the second and third floors will undergo renovation to add more group-study rooms and charging stations. During the four-week project, those floors will be closed to the public, but the first floor and the basement archives will remain fully open. To make up for the reduced seating, we have arranged additional study space in Room 120 of the neighbouring humanities building. Please also note that the library will temporarily close at eight in the evening rather than midnight, as construction crews need access after hours. We appreciate your patience, and we're confident the improvements will be well worth the disruption.

Attention, all commuters. Starting this Friday, the campus shuttle will follow a new route to better serve the east residence halls. The shuttle will now stop at the recreation center, the science quad, and the east dormitories before returning to the main gate. Please note that the stop at the old parking structure on Elm Street will be discontinued. Buses will run every fifteen minutes between seven in the morning and ten at night. Updated route maps are posted at every stop and available on the university mobile app. If you have questions about the new schedule, please contact the transportation office. Thank you, and enjoy the smoother ride.

Today we'll look at one of the most sophisticated communication systems in the animal kingdom: the waggle dance of the honeybee. When a foraging bee discovers a rich source of nectar, it returns to the hive and performs a remarkable figure-eight movement on the vertical surface of the honeycomb. This dance is not random—it encodes precise information about where the food can be found. Consider direction first. The bee waggles along a straight line, and the angle of that line relative to vertical tells the other bees the direction of the food relative to the sun. If the bee dances straight up, the food lies directly toward the sun; if it dances thirty degrees to the right, the food is thirty degrees to the right of the sun. Distance is conveyed by the duration of the waggle: the longer the waggling phase, the farther away the source. What's astonishing is that the watching bees can decode this information in the dark interior of the hive and then fly directly to the target, sometimes kilometres away. Researchers have even shown that bees adjust the dance to account for the sun's movement across the sky. This discovery earned Karl von Frisch a share of the Nobel Prize.

In this lecture, we'll explore the emergence of Impressionism in nineteenth-century France, a movement that overturned centuries of artistic convention. To appreciate why it was so radical, you must first understand the establishment it challenged. The official Salon, run by the French Academy, dictated what counted as serious art: historical scenes, mythological subjects, and highly polished surfaces in which no brushstroke was visible. Painters like Monet, Renoir, and Pissarro rejected all of this. Instead of grand narratives, they painted ordinary modern life—train stations, cafés, riverbanks on a Sunday afternoon. Instead of smooth, invisible brushwork, they applied paint in quick, broken strokes that captured the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere. Notice the priorities here: an Impressionist was less interested in depicting an object precisely than in recording the sensation of seeing it at a particular moment. This is why Monet could paint the same haystack dozens of times, each canvas registering a different quality of light. Critics were initially hostile—the very name Impressionism began as an insult, mocking Monet's loosely painted Impression, Sunrise. Yet within a generation, the movement had transformed Western painting and paved the way for the even more experimental art of the twentieth century. Their willingness to trust the eye over convention proved revolutionary.
The library closes early on Fridays.
My brother rides his bicycle to work every morning.
The teacher asked everyone to submit the report by Thursday.
We decided to postpone the picnic because the weather looked stormy.
The museum near the station offers free tickets to students on weekends.
Although the recipe seemed complicated at first, the finished dish turned out surprisingly delicious.
The volunteers gathered early in the park to plant young trees along the winding riverside path.
What is your favorite way to relax after a busy day, and why?
Describe a meal you enjoy cooking or eating, and explain what makes it special to you.
If you could visit any city in the world for a weekend, where would you go and what would you do there?
Talk about a hobby or activity you would like to learn in the future, and explain why it interests you.
Would you mind opening the window a little?
Do you know when the next bus arrives?
How do you like your new apartment?
Can you help me carry these boxes upstairs?
What did you think of the movie?
Are you free for lunch tomorrow?
I can't seem to log into my student account this morning.
This recipe calls for an ingredient I've never even heard of.
I'm not sure whether to take the morning class or the evening one.
The printer in the computer lab keeps jamming every time I use it.
I've been meaning to join a club, but I just can't decide which one.
Do you think this jacket will be warm enough for the hike?
I'd have signed up for the seminar earlier if I'd realized the spots would fill so quickly.
Much as I'd like to attend the conference, the registration fee is well beyond my budget.
Had I known the trail was closed, I'd never have driven all the way out here.
It's not that I dislike the course; it's just that the workload is far more than I bargained for.
Hi, I saw a posting for a part-time job at the campus dining hall. Is this the right place to apply?
Yes, this is the student employment office. I can help you get started. Are you currently enrolled at least half-time?
I am—I'm taking four courses this semester. How many hours a week would the job be?
Dining hall positions are usually ten to fifteen hours a week, and we schedule around your classes so it never conflicts with them.
That sounds manageable. Do I need any previous experience to apply?
No experience is required. The supervisor provides training during your first shift. You'll just need to fill out this form and show a photo ID.
Great. When would I be able to start?
Once your paperwork is processed—usually within three business days—the dining hall manager will call you to arrange your first shift.
Dr. Bennett, I wanted to talk about my statistics course. I'm seriously considering dropping it.
Let's think this through before you decide. What's prompting you to withdraw?
The pace is relentless, and I'm worried a poor grade will drag down my average. But I genuinely find the material useful.
If it's the grade that concerns you rather than the content, you might consider auditing instead of dropping. You'd attend the lectures without the mark counting toward your average.
I hadn't thought of that. But would auditing still let me sit the exams for practice?
You can do the coursework informally, but auditors don't receive an official grade, and the course won't count toward your degree requirements.
So if I ever needed the credit later, I'd have to take the whole thing again properly?
Precisely. My advice would be to stay enrolled a little longer. The withdrawal deadline isn't until next month, so you have time to see whether the workload eases after the midterm.
That's reassuring. I'll hold off on any decision until after the midterm, then.

Good morning, students and staff. The transportation office is excited to launch our new campus bike-share program starting next Monday. Docking stations with bicycles will be located at six spots around campus, including the library, the student center, and the science building. To use a bike, simply download the CampusCycle app, register with your student ID, and unlock any available bicycle by scanning the code on the handlebar. The first thirty minutes of every ride are completely free, and after that a small hourly rate applies. Helmets are available for checkout at the recreation center at no cost. We ask that you return each bike to a docking station when you're finished so that others can use it. Ride safely and enjoy exploring campus.

Attention, students in Professor Ramirez's chemistry class. Please note an important change regarding your final exam. The exam will no longer be held in our usual lecture hall, Room 214, because that building is undergoing electrical work that week. Instead, your final will take place in the Fairfield Testing Center, Room 108, on the ground floor. The date and start time remain the same: Friday at nine in the morning. Please arrive at least fifteen minutes early to find your assigned seat, and remember to bring your student ID and a calculator. If you have a scheduling conflict, contact the department office by the end of this week. Good luck with your studies.

Today we're going to explore something you've all seen but may never have fully understood: the rainbow. A rainbow appears when sunlight passes through raindrops in the air, and to grasp how it forms, we need three basic processes—refraction, reflection, and dispersion. When a ray of sunlight enters a spherical raindrop, it slows down and bends. This bending is called refraction. Now, sunlight looks white, but it's actually a mixture of all colors, and each color bends by a slightly different amount. Red light bends the least, while violet bends the most. This separation of white light into its component colors is called dispersion. Once inside the drop, the light reflects off the back surface and exits, bending once more as it leaves. The result is that each color emerges at a slightly different angle—red at about forty-two degrees, violet at about forty degrees. That's why we always see red on the outer edge of the arc and violet on the inner edge. And here's a curious fact: no two people ever see exactly the same rainbow, because the precise drops sending light to your eyes depend entirely on where you're standing.

When we think of defense in the natural world, we usually picture animals—claws, teeth, the ability to flee. But plants, being rooted in place, cannot run from the insects and herbivores that feed on them. Instead, over millions of years they've evolved an astonishing arsenal of chemical defenses. Broadly, these fall into two categories. The first is constitutive defenses—chemicals the plant produces continuously, whether or not it's under attack. Caffeine and nicotine are familiar examples; in high concentrations they're toxic to many insects. The second category is induced defenses, which the plant manufactures only in response to damage. When a caterpillar begins chewing on a leaf, the plant can detect the wound and rapidly ramp up production of bitter or poisonous compounds. What's truly remarkable is that some plants go further and release airborne chemicals—volatile signals—when attacked. These signals can do two things: warn neighboring plants to strengthen their own defenses in advance, and attract predatory insects that hunt the very herbivores doing the damage. In effect, the plant recruits bodyguards. So the next time you see a quiet field, remember it's actually a battlefield of invisible chemical warfare, waged silently by organisms we rarely credit with such sophistication.
The library opens at nine.
My brother rides his bicycle to school every morning.
Because the weather was so pleasant, we decided to eat our lunch outside.
The professor explained that the assignment would be due at the end of next week, not this one.
Although the recipe looked complicated at first, it turned out to be surprisingly easy once we gathered all the ingredients.
The museum's newest exhibit, which features photographs taken by ordinary citizens during the last century, has attracted far more visitors than the organizers had originally expected.

When the researchers compared the two neighboring towns, they discovered that the one which had invested heavily in public transportation reported not only cleaner air but also a noticeably stronger sense of community among its residents.
What is a hobby you enjoy doing in your free time, and why do you like it? Explain with details and examples.
Some people prefer to plan their days carefully, while others like to be spontaneous. Which do you prefer, and why?
Describe a place in your town or city that you would recommend to a visitor. Explain what makes it worth visiting.
If you could learn one new skill this year, what would it be, and how would it be useful to you? Give reasons and examples.
